Post_Text: I asked a superintendent four questions about her health plan. Who negotiated your pharmacy contract. Who picked your network. Who watches the Medicare order. Who does a teacher call the day she's diagnosed. She went quiet on all four. Here's what nobody had built: [LINK]

I asked a superintendent four questions about her health plan. She went quiet on all four. That silence cost her staff and her budget for six straight years. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

Your "best plan on the market" is one carrier, one network, one pharmacy manager, welded together and sold as a package. That's not a strategy. It's a product you bought six times. Here's what a real stack looks like instead: [LINK]

Post_Text: A teacher turns 65 and keeps working. On a self-funded plan, getting the order of payment wrong means your fund pays claims Medicare should have covered. Penalties included. Most brokers have never set this up once: [LINK]

The same procedure can cost 400 percent more at the hospital across town. Does your staff have one number to call and a real person to steer them? Or do they guess, and your fund eats the bill? This is the layer nobody builds: [LINK]

When a teacher hears the word cancer, who connects her oncologist to a top cancer center for a second read? The right diagnosis up front protects her life and your fund in the same motion. Here's how that gets built into a plan: [LINK]

An employee leaves and stays on COBRA. The ones who keep paying full freight are disproportionately the expensive ones. On a self-funded plan, that adverse selection lands on your fund, not a carrier's. Here's how to guard against it: [LINK]

Picture the health plan your staff have right now. Can you name who built each piece of it? Or did the whole thing just renew itself again while everyone was busy running a school? If you can't answer that, read this: [LINK]
